title: Introduction to OOP
learningObjectives: Explain Introduction to OOP with clear interview-ready wording
Implement Introduction to OOP using compilable Java examples for Day 7
Identify and prevent common bugs related to Introduction to OOP
Discuss design and performance trade-offs for Introduction to OOP
Solve at least one realistic exercise based on Introduction to OOP
Answer conceptual, coding, and scenario interview questions confidently

sections.title: Why Introduction to OOP matters
sections.content:
Introduction to OOP is a high-impact topic for Day 7. It directly supports Java fundamentals and programming basics and appears in interviews as a depth-check area.

Why this matters in practice:
- Teams rely on this concept for clean implementation decisions.
- Mistakes here create maintainability and production-risk issues.
- Strong understanding improves both coding rounds and system discussions.

Learning strategy for this day:
1. Define the concept clearly in one line.
2. Implement one minimal Java example.
3. Validate at least one edge/failure case.
4. Explain one trade-off (simplicity vs flexibility, speed vs safety, etc.).
5. Rephrase your answer as if explaining to an interviewer.

If you can explain Introduction to OOP clearly and implement it without copy-paste, you are interview-ready for this unit.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - Introduction to OOP
sections.content:
### Day 7 Topic: Introduction to OOP
This lesson belongs to Phase 1 (Java fundamentals and programming basics) and targets Beginner level expectations.

### Core Concept
Start by defining Introduction to OOP in precise terms, then connect it to runtime behavior and design impact.

### Internal Understanding
For this topic, interviewers expect more than API recall. They look for:
- correct mental model
- edge-case awareness
- clean implementation choices
- trade-off reasoning

### Practical Implementation Approach
1. Build a baseline solution that is correct and readable.
2. Add validation for boundary, null/empty, or failure scenarios.
3. Measure or reason about time/space or operational cost.
4. Refactor into maintainable units.

### Interview Structure (recommended)
- What is it?
- Why is it needed?
- One practical Java example
- One common pitfall
- One better alternative (if constraints change)

### Production Perspective
In real systems, correctness alone is not enough. Introduction to OOP should also support observability, safe changes, and team readability.

### Revision Checklist
- Can you explain this without notes?
- Can you code it from memory?
- Can you test one edge case quickly?
- Can you justify your approach in 30-60 seconds?

### Satyverse — deeper path (theory → practice)
**Class = blueprint; object = instance.** Constructors establish invariants; if you allow partial construction, bugs leak into every method.

**`this` and chaining:** fluent setters return `this` for readability — used in builders and test fixtures.

**Packages:** mirror directory structure; `public` types are your module boundary for other JARs.

**Inheritance preview:** prefer composition for “has-a”; use “is-a” sparingly — you will formalize this on Day 10.

**Practical OOP drill:** model a `BankAccount` with deposit/withdraw that cannot go negative — encapsulate balance as `private` and validate in one place.

sections.title: Basic Walkthrough
sections.description: Define a class with fields and a constructor — objects bundle state + behavior.

sections.title: Intermediate Implementation
sections.description: Inheritance + @Override: subtype replaces behavior while reusing the superclass contract.

sections.title: Advanced Production Pattern
sections.description: Polymorphism: store different subtypes behind Shape; dispatch calls the right area().

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Treating Introduction to OOP as memorization instead of implementation practice.
Skipping edge-case tests and validating only happy path.
Using unclear naming and tightly coupled method logic.
Giving interview answers without concrete examples.

sections.title: Exercise - Practice Introduction to OOP
sections.problem:
Implement a Java solution for **Introduction to OOP** with practical constraints.

Requirements:
1. Handle at least 5 test inputs.
2. Include one explicit edge/failure case.
3. Print deterministic output for verification.
4. Add short comments for decision points.
5. Keep the solution interview-readable and maintainable.
hints: Start with correctness first, optimize later.
Add one guard clause for invalid input.
Refactor method names before final answer.
sections.solution:
// Day 7 Introduction to OOP - reference solution intentionally omitted in prompt.
// Reuse the "Advanced Production Pattern" code block above as base and add edge-case handling.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is a class vs an object?
conceptual.answer: A class is a blueprint; an object is an instance with state in the heap.

conceptual.question: What is encapsulation?
conceptual.answer: Hiding internal state behind methods (getters/setters) to control invariants.

conceptual.question: What is a constructor?
conceptual.answer: Special method that runs on new; initializes object state; can be overloaded.

conceptual.question: What is this used for?
conceptual.answer: Refers to current instance; disambiguates fields from parameters; can delegate to other constructors with this(...).

conceptual.question: What is inheritance?
conceptual.answer: Subclass extends superclass, inherits accessible members, can override behavior.

conceptual.question: What is polymorphism?
conceptual.answer: Same interface or superclass type can refer to different subclass instances; overridden methods dispatch dynamically.

conceptual.question: What is composition vs inheritance?
conceptual.answer: Composition: class has-a dependency; inheritance: is-a relationship—favor composition when behavior varies.

conceptual.question: What is an abstract class?
conceptual.answer: Cannot be instantiated; may contain abstract methods subclasses must implement.

conceptual.question: What is an interface?
conceptual.answer: Contract of abstract methods (and default/static methods in modern Java) a class implements.

conceptual.question: What is Object superclass?
conceptual.answer: Every class extends Object implicitly; equals, hashCode, toString inherited.

conceptual.question: What is method overriding?
conceptual.answer: Subclass replaces superclass instance method with same signature; needs @Override for clarity.

conceptual.question: What is covariant return type in overriding?
conceptual.answer: Subclass method may return a subtype of the superclass return type.

conceptual.question: What is final on a class or method?
conceptual.answer: final class cannot be extended; final method cannot be overridden.

conceptual.question: What is instanceof used for?
conceptual.answer: Runtime type check before cast; pattern matching binds variable in newer Java.

conceptual.question: Why avoid God classes?
conceptual.answer: Single class doing everything violates cohesion; split responsibilities.

codeBased.question: Minimal class with field, constructor, getter.
codeBased.answer: private final String name; ctor sets; public String getName().

codeBased.question: Subclass extends superclass and calls super(...).
Demonstrate polymorphism: Animal a = new Dog(); a.speak();
Implement equals/hashCode contract sketch.
codeBased.answer: equals on business keys; hashCode consistent with equals.

codeBased.question: Interface with default method implemented by class.
codeBased.answer: interface Logger { default void log(String m){ System.out.println(m);} }

codeBased.question: Abstract class with abstract method.
codeBased.answer: abstract void draw(); concrete subclasses implement.

seniorScenario.question: Deep inheritance hierarchies in domain model—risk?
seniorScenario.answer: Fragile base class, hard refactors; prefer shallow trees or composition.

seniorScenario.question: Liskov substitution violation example?
seniorScenario.answer: Subclass throws on method superclass promised not to—breaks polymorphism expectations.

seniorScenario.question: Testing when logic is in subclass—approach?
seniorScenario.answer: Test each subclass behavior; use factories for complex graphs.

seniorScenario.question: Framework requires no-arg constructor—conflict with your design?
seniorScenario.answer: Use reflection-friendly patterns or provider factories; document constraints.

seniorScenario.question: Migrating from concrete inheritance to interface—steps?
seniorScenario.answer: Introduce interface, delegate old class, migrate callers gradually with adapter.

seniorScenario.question: equals symmetry broken in hierarchy—symptom?
seniorScenario.answer: a.equals(b) != b.equals(a)—fix by narrowing contract or using composition.

wrongAnswers: private methods can be overridden in subclasses.
You can instantiate an abstract class with new if you implement methods later.
static methods override instance methods with the same name.
Every class must explicitly extend Object in source code.
Polymorphism means the compiler picks overridden methods at compile time.
Interfaces can contain instance fields like classes.

sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Area | Rule | Interview Tip | Gotcha |
|---|---|---|---|
| Concept | Define Introduction to OOP in 1-2 lines | Start with purpose first | Vague definitions |
| Code | Write minimal compilable example | Explain expected output | Hidden edge-case bug |
| Validation | Test boundary + invalid input | Mention test strategy | Only happy-path tests |
| Trade-off | Compare 2 approaches | Link choice to constraints | "Depends" with no criteria |
| Phase Context | Align with phase 1 focus | Show progression mindset | Isolated-topic thinking |
